coredata.dc:title: Developing a switch “OFF-ON” fluorescent probe for detection of melamine based on doubly-protected red emissive copper nanoclusters mediated by Hg2+ ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ali, Ramadan
Alattar, Abdullah
Albalawi, Abdullah S.
Alkhamali, Alanoud
Hakami, Osama A.
Alharthi, Hamzah Hussain
Alahmari, Mohammed S.
Alharbi, Abdullah H.
Aljohani, Omar Mohammed
Yahya Alzahrani, Yahya A.
Albaqami, Turki M.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
coredata.dc:description: Melamine, often used as an adulterant in infants’ formula due to its high protein content, can be harmful when ingested in large amounts, leading to the formation of cyanurate-melamine co-crystals in infants and potentially causing kidney damage. In this study, we introduce a fluorescent method for the selective and reliable detection of melamine in milk and infants’ formula. The fluorescent probe comprises copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) functionalized with thiosalicylic acid (TSA) and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) as double-protecting ligands. Upon the addition of Hg2+, the fluorescence emission of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs is diminished due to static quenching. Subsequently, the fluorescence emission of the TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ probe is restored upon the introduction of melamine, facilitated by the coordination interaction between melamine and Hg2+ and the formation of a stable chelate between them. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescence emission was recorded initially for the TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ probe (F°) and after melamine addition (F). The (F/F°) ratio increased with rising melamine concentrations within the range of 0.025–65 µM. The detection limit, calculated using a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, was determined to be 8.0 nM. The TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ probe was successfully employed to detect melamine in milk and infants’ formula, yielding acceptable recovery percentages and relative standard deviations. These results underscore the reliability and efficacy of the proposed probe for the fluorometric detection of melamine in real-world samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Melamine
Thiosalicylic acid
Polyvinylpyrrolidone
Static-quenching
Milk and infants’ formulas

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of melamine using TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ probe

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs (insets are grain size distribution diagram and high resolution TEM); (B) XPS survey of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs; (C) Deconvoluted spectrum of Cu 2p; (D) FT-IR spectra of TSA (a), PVP (b), and TSA-PVP@Cu NCs (c).
(A) Excitation and fluorescence of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs while (B) is excitation-independent emission.

(A) UV/Vis absorption spectra of TSA, TSA@Cu, PVP, PVP@Cu, TSA/PVP, and TSA-PVP@Cu NCs; (B) Fluorescence spectra of PVP@Cu, TSA@Cu, and TSA-PVP@Cu NCs; (C) DLS of TSA, TSA@Cu, PVP, PVP@Cu, TSA/PVP, and TSA-PVP@Cu NCs.

The effect of (A) volumes of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs (50–300 µL), (B) 5.0 µM quencher type (Hg2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Sn4+, Cu2+), (C) Different concentrations of Hg2+ (0.35–10.0 µM), (D) pH value, (E) quenching time (1.5–4.0 min), (F) reaction time between melamine and TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ probe.
Fluorescence emission of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ probe in the presence of different concentrations (0.025–65 µM). Inset is calibration plot.
Selectivity and anti-interference studies of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ probe towards 300 µM interfering species and 10 µM melamine.
Detection of melamine using TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ probe and other fluorescent systems.
Detection of melamine in liquid milk and infants' formula using TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ probe (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Developing a switch “OFF-ON” fluorescent probe for detection of melamine based on doubly-protected red emissive copper nanoclusters mediated by Hg2+ ions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Melamine, often used as an adulterant in infants’ formula due to its high protein content, can be harmful when ingested in large amounts, leading to the formation of cyanurate-melamine co-crystals in infants and potentially causing kidney damage. In this study, we introduce a fluorescent method for the selective and reliable detection of melamine in milk and infants’ formula. The fluorescent probe comprises copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) functionalized with thiosalicylic acid (TSA) and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) as double-protecting ligands. Upon the addition of Hg2+, the fluorescence emission of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs is diminished due to static quenching. Subsequently, the fluorescence emission of the TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ probe is restored upon the introduction of melamine, facilitated by the coordination interaction between melamine and Hg2+ and the formation of a stable chelate between them. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescence emission was recorded initially for the TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ probe (F°) and after melamine addition (F). The (F/F°) ratio increased with rising melamine concentrations within the range of 0.025–65 µM. The detection limit, calculated using a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, was determined to be 8.0 nM. The TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ probe was successfully employed to detect melamine in milk and infants’ formula, yielding acceptable recovery percentages and relative standard deviations. These results underscore the reliability and efficacy of the proposed probe for the fluorometric detection of melamine in real-world samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Melamine, a trimer of cyanamide with a chemical formula of C3H6N6, finds extensive application across diverse industries, including pesticides, fire-retardants, and melamine/formaldehyde resins [1,2]. Notably, its substantial nitrogen content (66.6 % by mass) renders it susceptible to being used as an adulterant in milk and certain infant formulas, falsely boosting protein content [3]. Ingesting melamine in large quantities promotes the formation of cyanurate-melamine co-crystals in infants' kidneys, leading to kidney damage and potentially fatal consequences. The Dumas test and Kjeldahl method stand out as the most commonly employed conventional techniques for quantifying melamine [4]. Many analytical approaches were developed for estimation the content of melamine in milk and dairy product such as High Performance Liquid Chromatography with UV detection (HPLC/UV) [5], Gas Chromatography with mass detection (GC–MS) [6], chemiluminscence [7], Surface Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy (SERS) [8], near infrared spectroscopy [9], and electrochemical methods [10,11]. Among these methods, fluorometric-based sensing approaches have garnered significant attention due to their simplicity, selectivity, speed, and sensitivity [12–14].

Numerous fluorescent nanostructures have been engineered for detecting melamine, including carbon quantum dots [15], quantum dots (QDs) [16], metal–organic frameworks [17], and metal nanoclusters [18]. For instance, Zhang et al. developed water-soluble CdTe QDs for the fluorometric detection of melamine. Nevertheless, the high cost, toxicity, and challenges related to the optical properties of these QDs constrain their utility in sensing applications [19]. Metal nanoclusters (MNCs) have attracted considerable attention due to their notable Stokes shift, low toxicity, high fluorescence capability, and straightforward synthesis, positioning them as prime candidates for fluorescence-based sensing across a range of analytes [20–22]. Few fluorometric methods based on NCs were found in the literature for sensing of melamine. For example, Kalaiyarasan et al. developed ratiometric-based fluorescent probe for detection of melamine with detection limit of 0.28 µM [18]. Within the realm of MNCs, copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) stand out as a preferred choice over other precious MNCs. They are characterized by their ease of preparation, environmentally friendly nature, and cost-effectiveness [23]. However, a primary challenge associated with Cu NCs is their low stability, a hurdle that can be addressed by leveraging protective agents like DNA [24], proteins [25], thiolated ligands [26], and polymers [27]. Functionalized Cu NCs have been synthesized for the detection of Hg2+ ions and melamine. Zhang et al. developed cysteine-modified Cu NCs for the detection of Hg2+ and glutathione (GSH), achieving limits of detection (LOD) of 0.52 µM and 0.59 µM, respectively [28]. Li et al. designed L-penicillamine-functionalized Cu NCs, in combination with carbon dots, for ratiometric detection of Hg2+, with LOD of 0.31 nM [29]. Moreover, a DNA-templated Cu NCs probe has been proposed for the detection of melamine, achieving a LOD of 0.1 ppm [30]. Notably, no reports in the literature describe the synthesis of Cu NCs using TSA and PVP as dual protecting agents, which could enhance the fluorescence properties.

In this study, doubly-protected Cu NCs were engineered for the selective and sensitive detection of melamine. The red-emitting Cu NCs were crafted using copper as the metal core and thiosalicylic acid (TSA)/polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) as the reducing and stabilizing agents, employing a straightforward hydrothermal method. The introduction of mercuric ions (Hg2+) led to the quenching of the fluorescence emitted by TSA-PVP@Cu NCs. In the presence of melamine, the compound chelated Hg2+, thereby restoring the fluorescence emission of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs. This newly developed probe was successfully employed for detecting melamine in milk and various infants' dietary products.

Melamine (>98 %), lysine (98 %), isolysine (97 %), methionine (98 %), tryptophan (98 %), proline (98 %), serine (97 %), arginine (98 %), casein (98 %), glucose (>97 %), cysteine (>98 %), urea (>97 %), glycine (>98 %), phenylalanine (>98 %), ascorbic acid (>97 %), cholesterol (>98 %), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, >97 %), and thiosalicylic acid (TSA, >98 %) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Potassium chloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), HCl, NaOH, acetonitrile, mercuric nitrate (Hg2+), calcium chloride (CaCl2), and copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4 were procured from Merck. Ultrapure water was used along the whole research work. Liquid milk and infants' formula were procured from local markets.
Characterization and measurement instruments were listed in supporting information file.

Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) weighing 0.18 g was sonicated in 12.0 mL of ultrapure water for 10 min until complete solubility was achieved. Following this, 1.5 mL of CuSO4·5H2O (15.0 mM) was added slowly. Subsequently, 10.0 mg of TSA was introduced, and the dispersion was then heated to 80 °C for three hours. Upon cooling, the solution underwent centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 20 min prior to filtration. Lastly, the resulting solution was dialyzed using 2000 Da dialysis bags for 48 h and stored in a refrigerator until needed.

The TSA-PVP@Cu NCs probe (200 µL) was combined with 300 µL of 5.0 µM Hg2+ and 400 µL of phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). After incubation for three minutes at room temperature, 100 µL of varying amounts of melamine were introduced. Subsequently, the solution was further incubated for two minutes at room temperature before recording emission spectra at 660 nm following excitation at 300 nm.

2.0 mL of liquid milk and 2.0 g of dietary infants' formula were combined with 15 mL of ethanol. Subsequently, 1.5 mL of trichloroacetic acid (5 %) and acetonitrile (5 %) were added, followed by stirring for 15 min. The resulting mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for ten minutes to eliminate proteins. Finally, suitable quantities of melamine were introduced to 100 µL of the filtrate before proceeding with the determination steps outlined in section 2.4.

The as-fabricated TSA-PVP@Cu NCs probe displayed a spherical morphology, as confirmed by TEM analysis, with an average particle size of 2.1 ± 0.06 nm (Fig. 1
A). The inset in Fig. 1 A illustrates a high-resolution TEM image showing a lattice spacing of 0.19 nm, consistent with the synthesis of Cu NCs [31]. In Fig. 1 B, the XPS spectrum of the TSA-PVP@Cu NCs probe exhibits peaks at 183.4, 292.7, 389.3, 539.4, and 953.4 eV, which correspond to S 2p, C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Cu 2p, respectively [32]. The deconvoluted spectrum of Cu 2p reveals two distinct peaks at 931.7 and 953.5 eV, corresponding to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 1 C). The absence of a peak around 942.4 eV indicates the lack of the Cu2+ valence state, suggesting that only Cu° is present as the core metal [29]. Fig. 1 D presents the FT-IR spectra of TSA, PVP, and TSA-PVP@Cu NCs. In Fig. 1 D(a), the FT-IR spectrum of TSA displays absorption bands at 3270, 2817–2898, 2238, 1710, 1560, and 1290 cm−1, corresponding to υ (OH), υ (CH2), υ (SH), υ (CO), δ (OH), and υ (CO), respectively [33,34]. The absorption band at 2238 cm−1 was vanished after interaction with Cu NCs, suggesting the formation of CuS covalent bond. Fig. 1 D(b) illustrates the FT-IR spectrum of PVP with absorption bands at 2820–2905 cm−1 and 1695 cm−1, assigned to υ (CH2) and υ (CO), respectively. Fig. 1 D(c) represents the FT-IR spectrum of the TSA-PVP@Cu NCs probe. Fig. S1
A showcases the XRD pattern of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs, revealing diffraction peaks at 23.7°, 38.2°, 52.7°, and 69.4°, which can be attributed to the (002), (111), (200), and (220) planes, respectively [35]. Fig. S1
B displays the EDX pattern of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs with sharp peaks of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, and copper. The stability of the as-prepared TSA-PVP@Cu NCs was assessed under a range of conditions, encompassing different ionic strengths, irradiation times, and pH values ( Fig. S2 ). The results indicated that the as-prepared TSA-PVP@Cu NCs demonstrated a notable level of stability across various experimental parameters. Fig. 2
A delves into the excitation/fluorescence spectra of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs, with excitation and fluorescence wavelengths situated at 300 nm and 660 nm, respectively. The excitation-dependent emission spectra for TSA-PVP@Cu NCs were examined in Fig. 2 B, revealing that the emission wavelengths remain consistent regardless of the excitation wavelengths. This observation underscores the distinct characteristics possibly arising from the size and/or functional groups present on the fluorescent probe's surface [36,37]. The as-prepared TSA-PVP@Cu NCs has fluorescence quantum yield of 12.67 % (see supporting information for calculation of quantum yield).

In this study, the Cu NCs probe was synthesized through a one-pot chemical reduction process involving Cu2+ ions using TSA and PVP as ligands. Interestingly, it was observed that neither TSA nor PVP alone could generate fluorescent Cu NCs. The UV/Vis absorption spectrum of TSA@Cu in Fig. 3
A was virtually identical to that of TSA, while the absorption spectra of PVP and PVP@Cu were also indistinguishable. These results provided clear evidence that both TSA@Cu and PVP@Cu probes lacked fluorescence, as depicted in Fig. 3 B. Furthermore, Fig. 3 C illustrates the Zeta potential values of TSA, TSA@Cu, PVP, PVP@Cu, TSA/PVP, and TSA-PVP@Cu NCs. The Zeta potential values for TSA, PVP, and TSA/PVP were measured at −18.6 mV, −20.5 mV, and −14.7 mV, respectively. Upon the introduction of Cu2+ ions, these values shifted to −16.3 mV, −18.9 mV, and −28.9 mV in the same order, indicating slight changes due to electrostatic interactions. Notably, the addition of Cu2+ ions to a mixture of TSA/PVP resulted in a significant alteration in Zeta potential, suggesting a pronounced reduction of Cu2+ ions by these ligands to yield negatively charged Cu NCs rather than simply mixing, as shown in Fig. 3 B. The non-reducing nature of PVP is a well-established fact, rendering the PVP@Cu NCs probe unsuitable as a fluorescent platform. Conversely, it was discovered that TSA acts as a reducing agent capable of reducing Cu2+ to yield TSA@Cu NCs under alkaline conditions. When subjected to acidic conditions, the presence of COOH groups in TSA induces repulsion between molecules, causing the breakdown of TSA@Cu NC aggregates and subsequently resulting in the quenching of fluorescence [38]. Moreover, in acidic conditions, the inclusion of PVP serves to coat the shell, thereby shielding the aggregates from decomposition, thus enabling the successful creation of TSA/PVP@Cu NCs [39,40]. Additionally, the existence of lone pairs of electrons within TSA and PVP facilitates a coordination interaction with the vacant orbitals of Cu2+, validating the formation of TSA/PVP@Cu NCs [41]. In Fig. S3
A, the impact of synthesis temperature on the production of TSA/PVP@Cu NCs is elucidated. The investigation concluded that the optimal synthesis temperature is 80 °C. It was observed that elevating the temperature from 40 °C to 80 °C promoted fluorescence emission by enhancing aggregation, leading to aggregation-induced emission enhancement. However, surpassing 80 °C resulted in a decline in fluorescence emission due to the decomposition of the NCs, consequently diminishing the fluorescence intensity ( Fig. S3
B). Additional favorable synthesis parameters included 0.18 mg of TSA, 10.0 mg of PVP, and synthesis duration of three hours, as depicted in Fig. S3
(C–E).

The optimization of reaction variables significantly impacts the sensitivity of melamine detection. Therefore, it is crucial to fine-tune the parameters of the detection procedures, including the concentration and volume of the fluorescent probe, the type and concentration of the quencher, detection pH, quenching time, and reaction time (Fig. 4 ). In Fig. 4 A, the impact of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs volume on the detection process is illustrated. It was observed that increasing the volume of the fluorescent probe led to a rise in the fluorescence response from 50 to 200 µL. Beyond 200 µL, the fluorescence response of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs stabilized. Consequently, 200 µL of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs was identified as the optimal volume for melamine sensing. Fig. 4 B delves into the effect of quencher type (metal ion) on the fluorescence response of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs. Various metal ions, such as Fe3+, Al3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, and Sn4+ were tested based on the response of MNCs modified with the PVP ligand [42,43]. Mercuric ion (Hg2+) was identified as the most effective quencher, hence selected for melamine detection. The exceptional specificity is attributed to the stronger binding affinity between Hg2+ ions and SH groups of TSA, which facilitates non-radiative recombination of excitations via an efficient electron transfer mechanism [44,45]. Subsequently, the concentration of Hg2+ ion was analyzed within the range of 0.35–10.0 µM (Fig. 4 C). The optimal concentration of Hg2+ ion, providing the highest quenching effect, was determined to be 5.0 µM. Consequently, 5.0 µM Hg2+ was chosen as the optimal quencher concentration for the fluorometric detection of melamine. In Fig. 4 D, the impact of pH on the fluorometric detection of melamine is illustrated. The optimal pH value for melamine detection was determined to be 7.0. Below this value, the protonation of melamine's amino groups reduced the coordination interaction between Hg2+ ion and melamine. Above pH 7.0, the precipitation of Hg2+ impeded the coordination interaction between the ion and melamine. Fig. 4 E demonstrates the effect of quenching time between TSA-PVP@Cu NCs and Hg2+ ion within the range of 1.5–4.0 min. The optimal quenching time between the fluorescent probe and Hg2+ was found to be three minutes. Lastly, the impact of incubation time between melamine and TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ probe was investigated within the range of 0.5–3.0 min (Fig. 4 F). The optimal reaction time between the TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ probe and melamine was determined to be two minutes.

In Fig. S4
A, it is illustrated that the TSA-PVP@Cu NCs did not exhibit any fluorescence alterations upon the addition of melamine, underscoring the significance of introducing the quencher (Hg2+ ion) for melamine detection. Fig. S4
B presents the UV/Vis absorption of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs at 318 nm. This absorption spectrum experienced a blue-shift to 276 nm following the addition of Hg2+ ion, indicating the occurrence of static quenching between the TSA-PVP@Cu NCs and Hg2+ ion [46]. In Fig. S4
C, the fluorescence lifetimes of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs are depicted in both the absence and presence of Hg2+ ion, measured at 8.67 and 8.69 ns, respectively. These results suggest that the introduction of Hg2+ did not notably alter the fluorescence lifetimes, indicating the presence of static quenching [47–50]. The Stern-Volmer equation was utilized to validate the quenching mechanism of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs by Hg2+ ion, as shown in Fig. S4
D. The equation used to describe the quenching mechanism is as follows: (F°/F) = 1 + Ksv [Q], where F° and F represent the fluorescence emission of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs before and after the addition of Hg2+ ion, respectively. In this equation, Ksv denotes the quenching constant, and Q signifies the concentration of the Hg2+ ion (quencher). The data indicates that the quenching constant (slope) decreases with rising temperatures, indicating that the interaction between TSA-PVP@Cu NCs and Hg2+ is a form of static quenching [51,52]. To investigate the mechanism of fluorescence recovery of the TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ probe following the addition of melamine, a series of experiments were conducted. In Fig. S5
A, the UV/Vis spectra of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs after the addition of Hg2+ are presented. It is evident that the UV/Vis absorption band of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs underwent a blue shift upon the introduction of Hg2+. Upon the presence of melamine, the UV/Vis absorption spectrum of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ displayed a slight red shift without altering the peak's shape. These results indicate that melamine does not interact with TSA-PVP@Cu NCs but instead forms a complex with the Hg2+ ions. In Fig. S5
B, Zeta potential measurements for TSA-PVP@Cu NCs, TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+, and TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+/melamine are illustrated. The recorded values are −28.2 mV, −13.8 mV, and −18.6 mV, respectively. These results confirm that the introduction of Hg2+ led to a reduction in the negative charge of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs. Following the addition of melamine, it chelated the Hg2+ ions, effectively removing them from the surface of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of melamine using TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ probe
ce:section.ce:para: The TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ probe was utilized to detect melamine in a standard solution by measuring the fluorescence emission at 660 nm under optimal conditions. As depicted in Fig. 5 , it was evident that the fluorescence intensity increased proportionally with the concentration of melamine within the range of 0.025–65 µM. A linear calibration curve was established for melamine concentration, represented by the regression equation F/F° = 1.2 + 0.0415[melamine], exhibiting a high determination coefficient (r2) of 0.9983. The detection limit was calculated to be 8.0 nM, based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3:1. The results, encompassing linear ranges and detection limits of both the proposed and previously reported methods, are detailed in Table 1 . Overall, it was deduced that the developed probe offers notable advantages, including a wide-linear range and a low detection limit.

The reproducibility of the newly developed fluorescent probe was validated by fabricating ten distinct batches under identical conditions. The Relative Standard Deviation (RSD %) for the readings, falling below 3.67 %, underscores the consistency and reliability of the fluorometric methodology. Moreover, the selectivity and anti-interference studies of the fluorescent probe was assessed in the presence of various interfering species (300 µM), including inorganic species (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Fe2+, and Fe3+) and organic compounds (lysine, isolysine, methionine, tryptophan, proline, serine, arginine, casein, glucose, cysteine, urea, glycine, phenylalanine, ascorbic acid, and cholesterol), (Fig. 6 ). Notably, only melamine elicited a significant effect, enhancing the fluorescence response of TSA-PVP@Cu NCs due to the anti-quenching effect of melamine. These outcomes confirm the probe's selectivity in detecting melamine.

The applicability of the TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ probe for quantifying melamine in real samples like liquid milk and infant formula was explored using the standard addition method. As depicted in Table 2 , the recoveries % ranged from 98.5 % to 103.4 % with corresponding RSD % values between 2.67 % and 3.87 %. Furthermore, standard HPLC/UV methodology was employed to detect melamine in these samples. The comparison revealed minimal variance between the results obtained through the fluorometric method and the HPLC/UV method. These observations underscore the accuracy and precision of the developed fluorescent probe for melamine detection in real-world samples.

A robust fluorescent probe has been developed for the selective and reliable detection of melamine. This fluorescent probe was synthesized using a one-pot reduction method to create doubly-protected copper nanoclusters utilizing thiosalicylic acid (TSA) and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP). Upon the addition of Hg2+ ions, the fluorescence of the fabricated TSA-PVP@Cu NCs was quenched due to static-quenching effects. Subsequent introduction of melamine led to the recovery of fluorescence emission from TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ through chelation between melamine and Hg2+ ions. Notably, this fluorescent probe exhibited a strong anti-interference capacity, highlighting its suitability for use in complex matrices such as milk and infant formula. Comparative analysis between the results obtained using the TSA-PVP@Cu NCs + Hg2+ probe and the HPLC/UV method was conducted to validate its efficacy.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125286.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

RSC Adv.

Anal. Methods

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An upconversion fluorescence resonance energy transfer nanosensor for one step detection of melamine in raw milk
Talanta

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visual detection of melamine in raw milk by label-free silver nanoparticles
Food Control

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ping, M. Zhang, H. Li, S. Li, Q. Chen, C. Sun, T. Zhang, Visual detection of melamine in raw milk by label-free silver nanoparticles. Food Control, 23 (2012), pp. 191-197.
